rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF HANS HERBERT BARTSCH
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Executor, Peltners Second Motion For A Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Petitioner filed a 4th request for production of documents in connection with his 6 objections to the accounting initially filed by respondent in December 2016. The objections to the accounting have been continued multiple times and are scheduled for hearing on May 24. The Court does not intend to continue that hearing date. The 4th request suffers from some of the same overbreadth and burdens of the 3d set. To avoid that problem, the Court ordered the parties in open court (see transcript) to meet and confer before propounding any further document requests on the accounting issues in order to tailor the discovery to specific objections instead of requesting all documents for an entire year, for example, which looked more like a classic fishing expedition than meaningful discovery. The Court's order was made pursuant to CCP 2031.060 (b). On Saturday, March 17, petitioner sent a meet and confer letter which requested respondent explain the basis on which they were requesting certain reimbursements. The questions seemed to be more in the form of interrogatories rather than requests for documents. On Sunday March 18, petitioner filed a declaration with the Court regarding the previous day's meet and confer letter, and on Monday March 19, petitioner sent an e-mail to the Court's probate examiner stating there had been no response to Saturday's meet and confer letter. On Tuesday, March 20 petitioner served its 4th set of request for documents. On Friday, March 23, respondent replied to the meet and confer letter and explained the basis for a number of the requests for payment referred to by petitioner, and further mentioned that petitioner had not given the meet and confer process an opportunity to succeed, and requested that the request for documents be withdrawn. Petitioner failed to respond to that request and did not engage in any attempts to meet and confer. Many of the requests are overly broad and burdensome (see for example requests 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 9, 14, 15), and would include documents filed in court and documents prepared by petitioner in litigation which they would already have copies of. Some document requests had been addressed in respondent's reply to the meet and confer letter, but petitioner failed to follow up to see if any additional documents were still required (see for example, requests 10, 13, 19, 22 and 26). It is further worth mentioning that some of petitioner's major objections, such as a request by respondent to be reimbursed by the estate for fees and expenses incurred in the civil fraud litigation, are more in the nature of a legal objection and it is difficult to see how a voluminous production of documents on that issue could assist the Court in resolving that fundamental question. The Court does not have the time nor the resources to go through all the requests for documents and determine which ones are permissible, and if so, what specific documents are necessary, as opposed to "all" documents which could include all manner of irrelevant documents. That was for the parties to work out during the Court's ordered meet and confer process. Instead, petitioner filed its 4th Request for documents without affording respondent a reasonable time to respond to its "meet and confer" letter, and then ignore respondent's efforts to meet and confer. Accordingly, the motion for protective order is granted, and the Court awards sanctions against petitioner and his attorney for reasonable fees and costs incurred in connection with this motion. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, May 23, 2018.
